author: @Jcreek201
text: I'm not sure why WiFi is a useless feature...it was the main reason I went for the Plus vs. the Pro since I don't need the larger build volume.  Imagine a laser jet printer in 2024 where you had to manually load every document onto a thumb drive, walk it over and manually load it onto a printer.  You'd be laughed out of the industry.  I'd rather control everything from my PC, just like any other printer.  Seems like a common sense improvement.

replies.author: @kylerathdesign
replies.text: Probably just his preference. But yeah - I print on Wi-Fi (it’s also the reason that I haven’t really been able to get a Creality).

author: @FadedDreamzTv
text: you never applied a z offset the second time you checked the z offset it did nothing after it homes the z again it will go back to the previous position. If it took you 2.7mm using that as an example then you need to apply that z offset on the ABL screen.

author: @yoyofargo
text: These are good printers after you update the firmware. Though they really ought to come with rails instead of POM wheels.  Cameras are useful when your printer is in the other room or in your garage/shed so you can see if it's shooting spaghetti. I print a lot of ABS/ASA and would prefer not to get the ASA fume flu. I always slap a $10 webcam on my printers that run klipper for this reason.

author: @tombouie
text:
Thks & a Question:
      ??Is there a 3D printer precise enough to print legos??

author: @marcobassini3576
text:
Acceleration is measured in meters per second SQUARED (m/s2), not m/s. Do not confuse velocity (m/s) with acceleration (m/s2).
Wifi is extremely useful in a printer: being able to send a print job from your computer with a mouse click (as opposed to copy the file on a memory card, eject the card, go to the printer and insert the card, scroll through the files, ....) is priceless.
The marketing claims of speed (500 mm/s) and 4 digits acceleration are meaningless, well, they are as meaningful as knowing that your car can reach 240 km/h. In practice you will go on average at 50 km/h, and every car can do that.
If you print fast the quality of your prints will suffer. I prefer to add 2 hours to the print time and have a perfect piece, rather than have a poor piece (that will remain poor for the eternity) 2 hours before.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a mothers love is priceless - wifi is ok -but seriously just how hard is it to carry a memory card 2-3 feet and press a button.

In the 70s, TV remotes did not exist and it was fine. Later remotes became available and they displaced completely from the market the TV sets without remotes. Nobody today would ever consider buying a TV set without a remote. Would you? Seriously?

author: @MisterkeTube
text: Using a Klipper printer without network is like driving a car without turning the steering wheel.  Sure, it can move forward, but it's so limited that you'll never want to use the sd-card or usb stick again once you've tried Mainsail or Fluidd ...

author: @richards9260
text: the neptune 4 pro has a problem with consistency in tollerance. Have you printed a tollerance test or a cylinder and checked if the 4 plus prints perfectly round?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I haven't noticed anything mate - but then i wouldn't - it's a 3d filament printer printing plastic parts not a cnc lathe and I use it for models where 'perfectly round' isn't an issue.

replies.author: @richards9260
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering My mistake with "perfectly round". The neptune 4 has a mistake of 0.2mm. For mechanical parts this is not good enough. My ender 3 for comparisson has a mistake of 0.015mm. I am thinking of buying the 4 plus, but if i cant print any functioning parts without annoyance, i wouldnt buy it. So if you ever test it out i.e with testing the roundness of a cylynder, hit me up :)

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
I'm still using my first 3D printer, a Winbo SuperHelper 155L which has a print volume of 155x155x205mm.
The print volume is sufficient for most of my projects, but that may have to do with not considering projects which would require larger volumes.
At the time, for $200 it was a really good deal because it also has a 500mW laser next to the nozzle, for engraving, and even cutting thin material.
It doesn't have a heated bed nor networking, but for me the most important feature is reliability, and there has been no issue with that printer since I started using it 4 or 5 years ago, no clogged nozzle, no damaged prints, no maintenance even, which is why I haven't felt the urge to get another one, no matter how tempting Robert makes it!

author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d
text: I've never used it, but I think the general use case for cameras on 3d printers is to be able to monitor the print remotely and stop it if there's a problem. Like a baby monitor for your 3d printer. Obviously it also needs to be connected to a network to work this way. I'm not arguing for or against the extra complexity, just explaining why it's there and why high resolution isn't very important to its purpose. Just needs to be good enough to tell if your print is sliding all over the bed from adhesion problems, falling over from structural failure in the print, or balling up a giant plastic clog at the end of the nozzle, smoking or bursting into flames, or even just ran out of filament...obvious stuff like that.

author: @Barry.T.racing
text: Do you recommend as a first printer??

author: @Ben_EH-Heyeh
text:
Now that I have aquired an Ender 3 S1 Pro I realized the build plate is too small for the projects I have in mind.
To resolve this, I am planning to print another printer, The 100, which is 2 to 3 times faster than the Elegoo Neptune 4 at a similar price. Because I'm printing the printer myself, I can size it for my need, 300mm or 350mm is the goal.

replies.author: @MisterkeTube
replies.text: Sizing the 100 to 300mm sounds like a bad idea.  It's high speed and accelleration while still being 3d printed is mainly because of its smaller size.  When going bigger, you want metal and 350mm is really getting to the limits of corexy (belts are getting very long).  At sizes > 350mm you'll typically find bedslingers again or CroXY.

replies.author: @Ben_EH-Heyeh
replies.text:
@MisterkeTube Not a bad idea at all. There are already community members with 280mm and 320mm 100's.
I asked Matt about going bigger and potentially needing a customized PLA, like PolyMax, a stronger industrial grade PLA, to cope with vibrations of a bigger machine. Matt said "No. Just PLA."

author: @McRootbeer
text: I ordered the Neptune 4 plus after seeing all the great reviews on the 3 series and the improvements on the 4.  First video I saw was yours 👍.  The plus seems like the best value, trading some of the convenience that bamboo has - no assembly and "cup holder" features, for a larger build volume and lower cost, retaining the most important features- reliability, all metal direct drive hotend, auto bed leveling, klipper firmware, and even has accelerometers for input shaping.  Can't wait for it to arrive in November.  Here's hoping I can get to grips with it in time for Christmas 😅.
